topic: A person learns their childhood bully became a therapist
raw_output:
[story 1]

The mahogany gleamed under the soft gallery lights, a stark contrast to the memory of scraped knees and whispered taunts. Elias adjusted his tie, the silk feeling suddenly too tight across his collar. He stood near the reception desk, a knot tightening in his stomach with every measured breath. Across the room, Dr. Marcus Thorne—once the architect of his most awkward adolescence—was calmly guiding a nervous new patient through a complex metaphor. Elias watched the practiced ease in Thorne’s movements, the way his voice modulated, shedding all traces of youthful aggression. He wanted to walk over, to demand an explanation, but his feet remained rooted to the plush carpet. A low hum of internal resistance vibrated through him; he simply wasn't going to let this change shake his carefully constructed composure. He took a slow sip of water, forcing his gaze back to the polished floor.

[story 2]

"I don't understand why you think I'm the right person for this," I muttered, leaning against the cool marble pillar. My jaw was clenched so tightly I could feel the strain in my molars. Marcus, now Dr. Thorne, looked up from his notes, his expression patient, almost gentle. He didn't flinch at the sharpness in my tone. "Because you carry a significant amount of unresolved history, Elias," he replied, his voice a low, resonant baritone that held no judgment, only observation. I pushed off the pillar, taking a deliberate step forward, then stopping abruptly. The impulse to lash out, to remind him of every petty slight, warred with a sudden, unfamiliar desire to simply listen. I shook my head, a tiny, sharp movement. *No,* I thought fiercely. *I won't let him see that I’m still the same.*

[story 3]

The air in the consultation room smelled faintly of sandalwood and old paper. I sat rigidly on the edge of the leather armchair, my hands clasped so tightly my knuckles were white. Marcus leaned forward, his eyes, which I remembered as cold and challenging, now held a deep, unwavering empathy. "Tell me about the pressure you feel," he prompted softly. The words felt like sandpaper against my tongue. I swallowed hard, the dryness in my throat making the action difficult. I wanted to snap back, to dismiss his gentle probing with a cutting remark about his current profession. Instead, I focused on the pattern of the rug beneath my feet, counting the threads. This whole situation felt like a deliberate trap, and I refused to fall into the carefully constructed narrative he was weaving around me. I held my silence, a deliberate, immovable barrier.
